event_name: Lazarus Group npm Brandjacking Campaign / buffer-utilities
confidence_reason: Sonatype and JFrog both describe malicious buffer-utilities behavior, and the live npm packument still shows historical release timestamps even though the package is now held by a security placeholder. The package-version matrix for the broader campaign remains incomplete.

artifact_analysis.execution_trigger: npm lifecycle installation path; JFrog reports a postinstall loader that runs immediately after install
payload_behavior: collects hostname, username, operating system, home directory, and process arguments
creates a hidden .vscode directory in the user's home folder

lockfile_hunts: buffer-utilities in package-lock.json, pnpm-lock.yaml, or yarn.lock
buffer-util-extend, express-denv, jwt-path, webpack-patch, chai-as-patch, chai-beta, or react-next-dom in dependency trees
filesystem_hunts: hidden .vscode directory under the user home folder
marker files .pkg_history and .pkg_logs in temp or home directories
process_hunts: npm install or pnpm install invoking node setup.cjs --no-warnings
Node child process launched in detached mode after fetching a second-stage payload
ci_cd_hunts: dependency install jobs that resolve buffer-utilities before lockfile review
CI runners retaining npm tokens or cloud secrets in environment variables
registry_hunts: tarball URLs for historical versions returning 404 in live registry checks

open_questions: Need complete package-version matrix for the whole campaign.
Need to verify whether buffer-utilities artifacts remain mirrored in any registry cache or package archive.

defender_takeaways.detection: Look for buffer-utilities family names in lockfiles and install logs, then confirm whether the host ever ran node setup.cjs --no-warnings or created .pkg_history, .pkg_logs, or a hidden .vscode directory.
defender_takeaways.hunting: Treat install-time matches as high-signal because JFrog describes a postinstall loader that fetches and launches a second-stage backdoor immediately after package installation.
defender_takeaways.remediation: Remove the package from dependency graphs, rebuild lockfiles from known-good sources, and review the install host for secrets that were present during the install window.
defender_takeaways.prevention: Pin dependency sources, review new npm names that mimic trusted ecosystems, and block unapproved install-time scripts in developer and CI environments.

containment_complete: Dependency references to buffer-utilities and the related brandjacking names are removed or pinned to a reviewed allowlist.
The affected host or runner has no remaining install-time execution from setup.cjs.
eradication_complete: The hidden .vscode directory and marker files created by the loader are removed after preserving copies for forensics.
Any second-stage payload artifacts are identified or ruled out by host review.
recovery_complete: Lockfiles are regenerated from clean sources and installs succeed without invoking unexpected lifecycle scripts.
Relevant npm, SSH, cloud, and CI credentials exposed during the install window are replaced or otherwise invalidated from a clean machine.
closure_required: A clean re-install on a hardened environment confirms that the package graph no longer resolves to the malicious package names.
The registry artifact gap remains documented if no external archive copy of the malicious tarballs can be located.
